questions.question: What is the purpose of the <output> element in HTML5?
choices: To represent the result of a calculation or user action
To create a text output stream to the console
To define a print-only section of the page
questions.explanation: The <output> element represents the result of a calculation or user action. It is commonly used with forms to display computed results, such as the sum of two input values.

questions.question: Which attribute on the <output> element specifies which form controls contributed to the result?
questions.explanation: The 'for' attribute on the <output> element contains a space-separated list of IDs of the form controls that contributed to the calculated result. This creates a semantic relationship between the inputs and the output.

questions.question: Which event is commonly used on a <form> to update an <output> element in real time as input values change?
questions.explanation: The 'oninput' event fires immediately when the value of an input element changes (e.g., typing, moving a slider). It is commonly used on the <form> element to recalculate and update the <output> in real time.

questions.question: What is the default display behavior of the <output> element?
questions.explanation: The <output> element is an inline element by default, meaning it flows within the surrounding text content and does not start on a new line.

questions.question: The ___ attribute on the <output> element gives it a name for form submission, similar to input elements.
questions.explanation: The 'name' attribute on an <output> element allows its value to be included when the form is submitted, just like other form controls. The output's current text content is submitted as its value.

questions.question: The <output> element's default value can be reset using the form's ___ method or a reset button.
questions.explanation: When a form is reset (via a <input type="reset">, <button type="reset">, or the form's reset() method), the <output> element reverts to its default value, which is the text content it had when the page loaded.
